This IRC library is opinionated about the default encoding, in particular that it's better to use a rich, preferred encoding and to fail when than assumption is violated rather than provide an imperfect decoding and silently pass. I'm confident in this opinion as this code is being used with the default decoder in many environments with great success. I recognize that others prefer a more lenient default decoding, which is why the decoder is supplied and documented in the readme.

> Looks like the DBus has standarized on '\0'-terminated binary strings. Do you know any APIs that provide non-'\0'-terminated "ay"s?

Yes: every byte array that is not "basically a string", and some byte arrays that **are** strings too.

For an example of the former, the `Capabilities` property in BlueZ takes an arbitrary binary blob, presumably as defined by some Bluetooth specification. I doubt you can unilaterally put an extra zero on the end and expect it to still work.

```
# bluez-5.36/test/simple-endpoint

#Channel Modes: Mono DualChannel Stereo JointStereo
#Frequencies: 16Khz 32Khz 44.1Khz 48Khz
#Subbands: 4 8
#Blocks: 4 8 12 16
#Bitpool Range: 2-64
SBC_CAPABILITIES = dbus.Array([dbus.Byte(0xff), dbus.Byte(0xff), dbus.Byte(2), dbus.Byte(64)])
# JointStereo 44.1Khz Subbands: Blocks: 16 Bitpool Range: 2-32
SBC_CONFIGURATION = dbus.Array([dbus.Byte(0x21), dbus.Byte(0x15), dbus.Byte(2), dbus.Byte(32)])

...

#Channel Modes: Mono DualChannel Stereo JointStereo
#Frequencies: 32Khz 44.1Khz 48Khz
#CRC: YES
#Layer: 3
#Bit Rate: All except Free format
#VBR: Yes
#Payload Format: RFC-2250
MP3_CAPABILITIES = dbus.Array([dbus.Byte(0x3f), dbus.Byte(0x07), dbus.Byte(0xff), dbus.Byte(0xfe)])
# JointStereo 44.1Khz Layer: 3 Bit Rate: VBR Format: RFC-2250
MP3_CONFIGURATION = dbus.Array([dbus.Byte(0x21), dbus.Byte(0x02), dbus.Byte(0x00), dbus.Byte(0x80)])
```

For an example of the latter, `org.freedesktop.DBus.GetConnectionSELinuxSecurityContext()` returns a byte-array with no trailing `'\0'`.

@dabeaz I was thinking you wouldn't like it because it would force every call to `sendall` to yield to the kernel, which was a point we disagreed on before :-). Probably also relevant to note though that that trio issue is talking about Trio's higher-level stream interface, the equivalent of `curio.SocketStream` rather than `curio.io.Socket`. (However, `trio.SocketStream.wait_send_all_might_not_block` is of course implemented using `trio.socket.SocketType.wait_writable`.)

Probably the simplest approach to doing this in curio would be for operations like `sendall` to become first-class traps. The alternative would be for the set of traps to vary on different systems and code like `sendall` would have to decide which traps to call depending on what OS it's running on, which seems like it might be more awkward.

Also FYI in case it's useful: asyncio has a Python wrapper for IOCP inside it. In the source it's `overlapped.c`; I don't have a windows machine handy so I'm not sure where it ends up (maybe `import asyncio.overlapped`?). I don't think it's considered public API, but for a proof-of-concept or as a starting point then it's probably quicker than writing a new wrapper from scratch :-). (In the long run I guess we probably would prefer a cffi wrapper for PyPy-friendliness, but that's low priority.)

> Will is being founded as a company, and with the core chat bot product, we'll have both the open-source library and a PaaS/SaaS service (ala Wordpress.com/.org). The core of the company is around an idea we call Personal AI.

You need to start thinking about open source governance and what it means to your organization. It's not enough to slap an MIT/BSD/Apache license on the repo and think you'll continue to attract developers who aren't being paid, yet are lining your pockets with money. This is especially true if my fears are realized - that you are going to convert what is effectively a standalone program you can run entirely independently (will) to a thinner client that *requires* a service-based offering to operate. (I'm sure you're aware that you've already got a lot of competition in this space.)

As an Apache Software Foundation member, and a member of one of its project's Project Management Committees (Apache CouchDB), I have an admitted bias towards what the ASF has done - but there are other examples out there like the Linux Foundation, X.Org, etc. Our project is a great case study. Apache CouchDB has been incorporated into IBM/Cloudant's cloud-offering Cloudant. They provide a superset of functionality and a fully managed service that provides almost everything you can do by running CouchDB standalone. Just upload your data and you're ready to go. But you can very easily stand up your own server(s), replicate your data off of Cloudant and do most of the same things yourself, via the open source software. And over time, features that were exclusive to Cloudant have been open sourced, like the full-text and geospatial search indexes.

Some links on the ASF approach:
* https://www.apache.org/foundation/how-it-works.html
* http://www.zdnet.com/article/apaches-open-source-governance-model/
* http://communityovercode.com/2015/03/how-apache-really-works/

I guess the point I'm trying to make is that while EHF funding may gate your ability to get started, and sustaining your passion project via a self-started company may be fulfilling, without a long-term strategy on the open source front that respects both your code contributors and your users, and doesn't force them into a services-related model, you will bleed volunteer developers, and potentially users as well.

The cause of the problem was determined. Leonardo boards, unlike Uno boards continue sending update data after the serial port is closed. PyMata now sends a Firmata Reset command to the Arduino so silence it before closing the port. To handle the case of a user pressing control-c before the application finishes processing, a signal handler should be added to the application. PyMata handles the case where control-c is pressed before __init__ completes.

All examples will be updated to include this signal handler.

The signal handler that optionally can be added to applications to gracefully handle control-c is:

```
board = None

# create a PyMata instance
board = PyMata("/dev/ttyACM0")

def signal_handler(signal, frame):
    print('You pressed Ctrl+C!!!!')
    if board != None:
        board.reset()
    sys.exit(0)

signal.signal(signal.SIGINT, signal_handler)
```

```

So I looked at this.  If you pop up an image_window and look at each of the images produced by the random_cropper you will see that sometimes the player is very near the edge of the image.  You have to realize that the cropper is cropping out images from your training image.  They aren't all going to be centered on the players.  In particular, the random cropper will pick background images with some chance and if that happens to contain your guy he could appear anywhere.  The cropper will mark objects as ignored if they are partly outside the crop, which normally prevents you from getting this error.  However, since your player is so small the center of the player can get close to the edge of the image, and, due to the way the CNN downsamples an image some of the border pixels are lost, putting the guy's center of mass outside the image.  

Anyway, I don't really know what to do about this other than to tell users to be careful about the min-batches they create.  You should look at the images (use image_window and plot the boxes to see what is happening) and see if it looks like reasonable training data.  It should also be emphasized that you need to label all the players in the image or training will be messed up.  It will try to learn that all the other players aren't players.  Which brings me to another point, you might be thinking that you can disable the random background cropping to avoid this whole issue, but you would still have a problem of a crop that happens to contain multiple players in view, which obviously all can't appear in the center of the crop.

I could just remove that error message and make the training automatically ignore such boxes.  Which is fine from a machine learning point of view and would be a fine thing to do in your specific case.  However, I have a pretty strong conviction that doing so would lead to users creating really bad training sets that are getting silently ignored by the algorithm, which would lead to the algorithm not learning a sensible result, or anything at all since it might ignore all the data.  

As for what you can do, you could add a check inside the loop that generates the mini-batch for boxes that are very close to the edge of the image and mark them as ignore by setting mmod_rect::ignore to true.  This is easy to do with an if statement something like 
```
if (!get_rect(img).contains(grow_rect(some_player,10))) 
    some_player.ignore = true;
```

As an aside, you should probably use tighter boxes around the players and make them higher resolution.  When you look at the crops coming out of the random cropper you will see that they players are little more than small blurs, which is another reason why you should always look at the training data you are making.

@jeffjzx  Proxying (perhaps through a load balancer, perhaps just nginx reverse proxy) is very common, and in those cases you pass the token in a HTTP header in each request, and the server verifies the token for every request. As @rjprins said, there is _no state_ involved, so no need for redis/database/globals.

As I understood it, your original question was about decoding a JWT twice when handling a request. I assumed the first time would be something like a `@login_required` decorator, and the second time you actually need some data field from the JWT. In that case my suggested approach works just fine: in the `@login_required` decorator (or similar; this depends on your web framework) you decode and verify the token, and you store the decoded result in a request-scoped "global". For flask `flask.g` would be the obvious choice. Within your view function (or "request handler"; depends on your web framework), you can simply access the previously decoded and verified data.  And since this data is kept in a request-scoped variable, it will be gone after the response has been sent back to the client, so the next request handled by your application will be completely independent (no shared state).

What I'm thinking more generally, trying to not to be Gmsh-centric, is that some input or output formats will store regions as *element -> region* and some as *region -> element*.  (I'm not imagining any other possibilities than these two, though I did try to.)  

It's true that we don't know of any mesh formats that support *region -> element-index* with elements being allowed to belong to more than one region, Exodus II with its Element Blocks does more work in the direction of *region -> element* than *element -> region*.

Another reference for the format (especially  ‘Anatomy of an Exodus II file’ in §3):

* Kostka, T. D. (2013, January). Exomerge user's manual: A lightweight Python interface for manipulating Exodus files. Technical Report [SAND2013-0725](http://prod.sandia.gov/techlib/access-control.cgi/2013/130725.pdf), Sandia National Laboratories, Livermore, California.

But anyway, not tying meshio too closely to any existing format, I wondered about whether the format-neutral data-structure that meshio builds and populates when called to read and uses when called to write, what if it had both possibilities?
1. *element -> region*, say implemented as at present in [`cell_data[type]['gmsh:physical']`](#199)

Then in `meshio.*_io.read`, if reading:
1. a type 1 format such as Gmsh MSH, populate `cell_data[type]['gmsh:physical']`
2. a type 2 format, populate `cell_data['regions']`

1. `cell_data[type]['gmsh:physical'] and we have to write
   1. a type 1 format, that's easy, already implemented
   2. a type 2 format, do the inversion.  Go through the `cell_data[type]['gmsh:physical'] and construct the maps *region -> element*

2. `cell_data['regions']` and we have to write

   2. a type 2 format, it should be straightforward.

I don't think you will be able to have a google map in a tkinter window, unless you can somehow embed html. Here's a minimal example that demonstrates using a `Tk` window to show a map in the notebook: ```python import tkinter import gmaps from IPython.display import display app = tkinter.Tk() def show_map(): m = gmaps.Map() display(m) button = tkinter.Button(app, text='show map', command=show_map).place(x=10, y=90) app.mainloop() ``` That said, I think that you're going to have trouble going down that road: you're mixing two different GUI engines, and notebooks are probably not the right place from which to launch a `Tk` event loop. I would recommend sticking entirely to jupyter widgets. By combining *jupyter-gmaps* with [default widgets](https://ipywidgets.readthedocs.io/en/latest/examples/Widget%20List.html), you should be able to build an app that lives entirely in the notebook.

import bt
%matplotlib inline

# download data
data = bt.get('aapl, msft, c, gs, ge', start = '2010-01-01')

# calculate moving average DataFrame using pandas' rolling _mean
import pandas as pd
# a rolling mean is a moving average
sma = pd.rolling_mean(data, 50)

# let's  see what the data looks like - this is by no means a pretty chart, but it does the job
plot = bt.merge(data, sma).plot(figsize=(15, 5))

class SelectWhere(bt.Algo):
    """
    Selects securities based on an indicator DataFrame.
    Selects securities where the value is True on the current date (target.now)
    Args:
            * signal (DataFrame): DataFrame containing the signal (boolean DataFrame)
    Sets:
            * selected
    """
    def __init__(self, signal):
        self.signal = signal
        
    def __call__(self, target):
        # get signal on target.now
        if target.now in self.signal.index:
            sig = self.signal.ix[target.now]
            
            # get indices  where true as list
            selected = list(sig.index[sig])
            
            # save in temp - this will be used by the weighing algo
            target.temp['selected'] = selected
        
        # return True because we want to keep on moving down the stack
        return True


# first we create the Strategy
s = bt.Strategy('above50sma', [SelectWhere(data > sma),
                               bt.algos.WeighEqually(),
                               bt.algos.Rebalance()])

# now we create the Backtest
t = bt.Backtest(s, data)

# and let's run it!
res = bt.run(t)

res.display()

def above_sma(tickers, sma_per = 50, start = '2010-01-01', name = 'above_sma'):
    """
    Long securities that are above their n period
    Simple Moving Average with equal weighs,
    """
    # download data
    data = bt.get(tickers, start = start)
    sma = pd.rolling_mean(data, sma_per)
    
    # crteate strategy
    s = bt.Strategy(name, [SelectWhere(data > sma),
                            bt.algos.WeighEqually(),
                            bt.algos.Rebalance()])
        
    # now we create the backtest
    return bt.Backtest(s, data)

# simple backtest to test long-only allocation
def long_only_ew(tickers, start = '2010-01-01', name = 'long_only_ew'):
    s = bt.Strategy(name, [bt.algos.RunOnce(),
                           bt.algos.SelectAll(),
                           bt.algos.WeighEqually(),
                           bt.algos.Rebalance()])
    data = bt.get(tickers, start = start)
    return bt.Backtest(s, data)

# create the backtest
tickers = 'aapl, msft, c, gs, ge'
sma10 = above_sma(tickers, sma_per = 10, name = 'sma10')
sma20 = above_sma(tickers, sma_per = 20, name = 'sma20')
sma40 = above_sma(tickers, sma_per = 40, name = 'sma40')
benchmark = long_only_ew('spy', name = 'spy')

# run all the backtest
res2 = bt.run(sma10, sma20, sma40, benchmark)

res2.plot()

res2.display()

class WeighTarget(bt.Algo):
    """
    Sets target weights based on a target weight DataFrame.
    Args:
        * target_weighs(DataFrame): DataFrame containing the target weights
    Sets:
        * weights
    """
    def __init__(self, target_weights):
        self.tw = target_weights
    
    def __call__(self, target):
        # get target weights on data target.now
        if target.now in self.tw.index:
            w = self.tw.ix[target.now]
            
            # save in temp - this will be used by the weighing algo
            # also dropping any na's just in case they pop up 
            target.temp['weights'] = w.dropna()
            
            
        # return True because we want to keep on moving down the stack 
        return True


## download some data & calc SMAs
data = bt.get('spy', start = '2010-01-01')
sma50 = pd.rolling_mean(data, 50)
sma200 = pd.rolling_mean(data, 200)

## now we need to calculate our target weight DataFrame
# first we will copy the sma200 DataFrame since our weights will have the same structure
tw = sma200.copy()
tw[sma50 > sma200] = 1.0
tw[sma50 <= sma200] = -1.0
# here we will set the weigh to 0 - this is because the sma200 needs 200 data points before
# calculating its first point, Therefore, it will start with a bunch of nulls(NaNs).
tw[sma200.isnull()] = 0.0

# plot the target weights + chart of price & SMAs
tmp = bt.merge(tw, data, sma50, sma200)
tmp.columns = ['tw', 'price', 'sma50', 'sma200']
ax = tmp.plot(figsize = (15, 5), secondary_y = ['tw'])

ma_cross = bt.Strategy('ma_cross', [WeighTarget(tw),
                                    bt.algos.Rebalance()])
t = bt.Backtest(ma_cross, data)
res = bt.run(t)

Actually the usage has made it clear that the URL should be put at the first position:

```bash
$ python httpstat.py
Usage: httpstat URL [CURL_OPTIONS]
       httpstat -h | --help
       httpstat --version
```

The time I was considering the URL's position, I was thinking that httpstat itself actually has no option. So it should be distinguished from normal situation, where intuitively options are put after command and before the main arguments. Thus when you see `httpstat URL [CURL_OPTIONS]`, it becomes very clear that the curl options are just for curl, and will definitely have no effect on how httpstat will perform. All the options for httpstat, are environment variables, separately.

In conclusion, the position of URL won't be changed, thanks for your suggestion and cooperation :)

P.S. it's easy to modify the code for your own to make URL at the last position, just change [L180](https://github.com/reorx/httpstat/blob/master/httpstat.py#L180) to `url = args[-1]` and [L88](https://github.com/reorx/httpstat/blob/master/httpstat.py#L188) to `curl_args = args[:-1]`.

@harvimt,
> Can you use `QtCore.QEventLoop`?

Running second event loop actually works in my configuration exactly as you suggested:

```python
class MainWindow(QMainWindow):
    ...
    def closeEvent(self, event):
        qloop = QtCore.QEventLoop()
        loop = quamash.QEventLoop(qloop)
        result = loop.run_until_complete(f())
```

But I don't think it's guaranteed behaviour: I/O primitive should be registered in an event loop, and I think it's not safe to use single I/O primitive in multiple event loops.
In my configuration the event loop uses `select.select`-based I/O demultiplexing, which allows using single I/O primitive in mutliple `select.select` calls, but I think this is not true for other selectors or when `asyncio.ProactorEventLoop` is used.

@horazont, you are right that only calling `super().closeEvent()` will cause window to close, but allowing return from my overridden `closeEvent()` leads to increase of program logic: I need to handle case when `closeEvent()` is called multiple times, and I'm not sure that I can store `event`, because Qt should destroy (or reuse) it while event processing finished (when `closeEvent()` handler returned).

In general I need what you, @horazont , suggested in PR #11 : `inline_async`, and it's implementation is practically that you, @harvimt , suggested, but as I stated before I think such implementation is not guaranteed to work on any low-level event loop implementation.

The key part you are missing is that objects is a dictionary of nested object dictionaries. In the description for objects: "Keys are the object names with values as Objects Dictionary." Basically,
there can be multiple objects for a result, especially when the depth value is larger.

I do need to fix the description for Objects Contact - address, email, and phone. Those fields are actually lists of dictionaries (since you can have multiple address, email, and phone entries per object).

So, to get the address for ABUSE5250-ARIN, your code would be something like:
`contact_address = tab_add(newline_clean(results['objects']['ABUSE5250-ARIN']['contact']['address'][0]['value']))`

That is simplified for the sake of the example. address, email, phone could be None:
```
tmp = results['objects']['ABUSE5250-ARIN']['contact']['address']
if tmp is not None:
    tab_add(newline_clean(tmp[0]['value']))
```

The above still just gets you the first contact address result. Depending on how you are displaying your data, you may want to display all the listed addresses. To do that, you will need to change how you are displaying your data, and iterate the address, contact, phone:
```
tmp = results['objects']['ABUSE5250-ARIN']['contact']['address']
contact_addresses = []
if tmp is not None:
    for addr_dict in tmp:
        contact_addresses.append(addr_dict['value'])
```

Does that make sense? I would greatly appreciate any help or suggestions on how to simplify the documentation for this.

I'm not sure if this description is quite the best way to think about this.  Here is how I'm thinking about it:

One set of weights would be used for all calculations involving positions.  Specifically the weighted centroid of every cell: xc = Sum(w x)/Sum(w), yc = Sum(w y)/Sum(w).

Another set of weights would be used for the products used for the correlations: Sum(wa wb ea eb) / Sum(wa wb).

So different weights for the two kinds of quantities that get weighted.  I think this is essentially what you are asking for.

```.pyx
cimport randomgen.threefry
import randomgen.threefry
from randomgen.distributions cimport brng_t

from libc.stdint cimport uint64_t

from cpython.pycapsule cimport PyCapsule_GetPointer

def myf(r):
    cdef brng_t *rng
    cdef uint64_t i
    cdef const char *name = 'BasicRNG'
    rng = <brng_t *> PyCapsule_GetPointer(r.capsule, name)
    i = randomgen.threefry.threefry_next64(<randomgen.threefry.threefry_state *>rng.state)

    return i
```

@will133 I looked into implementing removal of an object from a collection like that a while back. Can't remember the object off the top of my head, but it was like this, a member of a collection. I don't remember the details without repeating the research, but I came to the clear conclusion, as I recall, that using the `del` statement was going to be a bad idea for something like this.

The del statement is really designed for removing names from the namespace and it's surprisingly complex to implement it in a robust way, things like reference counts and so on making it a crap shoot on whether you ever receive a `__del__()` call and where.

But I do like using a slice for elegant specification; the method could use a variable-length argument list to allow specifying a single slide, a number of individual slides, or a list (sequence) of slides to be deleted. Something like:

```python
def delete_slides(*slides):
    ...
```

so you can call it like:

```python
delete_slides(slide_x)
```

or

```python
delete_slides(slide_x, slide_y)
```
or

```python
delete_slides(*prs.slides[1:3])
```

Hi Andreas,

I do get your opinion but I don't think kmeans is the right way to go here. I mean you usually don't "see" the number of colors from an arbitrary image that you should know as kmeans input in order to get a good result. I've tried the site you pointed out and added an example how you could add that palettes as well. Even with the white between the colors you're still a factor of six from the six palette colors to the background. 

For real image I'd use a different approach but I'm quite skeptic that there is a good way. If you've a lot of color variations the kmean clusters will have a large spread and the colors you get out might not be what you want. Nevertheless, I had to give it a try. At https://github.com/nicolajkirchhof/PaletteFromImage  you'll find a small kmeans palette extraction example. 

In contrast to kmeans I'd suggest using a hierarchical clustering that works with a color diff. This would prevent such extreme color changes. Sure, it might create a lot of colors initially but depending on the cluster size you could rank them and select only the most common ones. Maybe I'll give that a try the next days for now I found my simple solution rather appealing. ;)

All the best,
Nico 

PS: I don't think there is a "right" approach. With clearly defined color palette images you'll be fine with or without clustering and with custom images you'll have do some manual calibration, I think.

I think @cdce8p  can give a more in-depth example from the Home-Assistant community, but here is a quick one (just added the async keyword in the run method, otherwise its just the sensor from the accessories folder):

```python
import random

from pyhap.accessory import Accessory
from pyhap.const import CATEGORY_SENSOR


class TemperatureSensor(Accessory):

    category = CATEGORY_SENSOR

    def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
        super().__init__(*args, **kwargs)

        serv_temp = self.add_preload_service('TemperatureSensor')
        self.char_temp = serv_temp.configure_char('CurrentTemperature')

    @Accessory.run_at_interval(3)
    async def run(self):
        '''This decorator will basically await asyncio.sleep(3) before re-running this code'''
        self.char_temp.set_value(random.randint(18, 26))
```

Basically, if the `run` method of the `Accessory` is marked `async`, it will not run in a separate thread, but in the event loop. I.e. you can use asyncio in the `run` as it is ran in the event loop.

You can queue "jobs" in the event loop by calling `self.driver.async_add_job` within the Accessory (the method is in accessory_driver.py, go ahead an have a look).

Ok, so basically this seems to be a feature. I'm not sure when its helpful but it's being done on purpose. `quote_completions` escapes all characters that bash splits words on. It gets this from `environ['_ARGCOMPLETE_COMP_WORDBREAKS']` or `environ['COMP_WORDBREAKS']` if none of those are set it falls back to `" \t\"'@><=;|&(:."`.

Now, I'm not sure if that default set is sensible because I don't know the implications of, say, not escaping `'='`. Maybe @kislyuk has a better grip on this than me.

In the meantime you can either overwrite the `quote_completions` in a subclass of `CompleterFinder` or add on of the environment variables mentioned above.

Regarding code changes to argcomplete, it's probably worth having the default set as an attribute to make it easier to overwrite. Again I'dd like @kislyuk's input on this before doing a PR.

By the way, to clarify my earlier statement, the only "changed" behavior is the following corner case:
```python
mycwd = local.cwd
with mycwd('newdir'):
    print(mycwd)
```

Under the old system, mycwd would be the 'newdir' directory, but the new system it will still print the old dir. It does not really track the current working directory even in the old system, as any other changes to cwd through other methods will not change it. The new behavior, IMO, is actually more intuitive; and the other recommended methods work as expected:

```python
with local.cwd('newdir'):
    print(local.cwd)
```
This prints the new cwd, since cwd is really a property that gets the cwd. If you want the old behavior in a more pythonic way, you can even do:

```python
with local.cwd('newdir') as mydir:
    print(mydir)
```

Instead, we could define an `OrderedExecutionExperiment` (or something) which is a subclass of `Experiment`. Then we can abstract this part of `conduct` into `self._generate_variant_functions` (or something):

```
        # otherwise, let's wrap an executor around all of our functions and randomise the ordering

        def get_func_executor(obs_def, is_control):
            """A lightweight wrapper around a tested function in order to retrieve state"""
            return lambda *a, **kw: (self._run_tested_func(raise_on_exception=is_control, **obs_def), is_control)

        funcs = [
            get_func_executor(self._control, is_control=True),
        ] + [get_func_executor(cand, is_control=False,) for cand in self._candidates]

        random.shuffle(funcs)

```

That function would return a list of the functions to execute in order, and whether they're shuffled or not depends on how `self._generate_variant_functions` is implemented.

Thanks for the clueful comments, all. Weighing in here:

* I want to keep 2.7 support for now, as a lot of my own stuff is 2.7 as well.
* I think JS and Python may have similar enough regexes to tell people "Just make sure you write a compatible subset if you're targeting both JS and Python."
* I'm not sure how Transcrypt handles the chunks of the stdlib it does support, but it seems like the cleanest way to make Parsimonious compatible with it is to add support to Transcrypt for the few stdlib things we use. isfunction and ismethod ought to be portable, and getargspec should be technically possible as well (worst case, we have to look at the introspected source of a function). OrderedDict is pretty much just an ES6 Map, and version_info is a constant. exc_info depends on to what degree Transcrypt supports exceptions. The only thing we use ast for is interpreting \n, \t, etc. within Parsimonious strings. So really, the question is how easy it is to add new stdlib routines to Transcrypt.

I'm working on a python project that needs to consume SVG files, and ideally I would like to be able to traverse the original group structure of the SVG after the file is parsed, but still have convenience functions to flatten out the paths from the groups that they're nested inside of.

There are other python SVG parsing libraries that seem to handle the group transforms, but their APIs for traversing the group structure as a user seems rather lacking. Based on what I've seen, I think the `svgpathtools` library has the most comprehensive design, and the code in the `ElementTree` branch looks promising.

I think the one design change I would propose to `ElementTree` would be to add a `Group` class, which would allow for a more natural tree of `Group`s (It looks like the current design intends to have a tree of `Document` objects?). Then for the `Document` class, I can imagine three choices:

1. Have `Document` extend the `Group` class, and make all the ungrouped paths part of the `Document` group.
2. Give the `Document` class a default anonymous `Group` instance with no attributes that would contain  all the ungrouped paths.
3. Store any ungrouped paths in a list held by the `Document` class. I think this would be my preferred approach.

I'm happy to make these changes, implement the missing features, and write unit tests for all the currently untested features if you'd be on board with this idea. Since I have deadlines to meet, I'll probably move forward on this very soon (with or without your buy-in), but ideally I would like to submit any progress that I make upstream to you.

Disclaimer: I'm rather new to python (my expertise is in C++), so when I get around to submitting a pull request, there may be some novice python-specific issues that need correcting.

This is not true with Python 3.

Your PIN value is not a valid UTF-8 string (as requested by PKCS#11 specification):
```
>>> pin = b'\xbf\xa7\x91\x19v\x07\x9fkuz2\xd6\x9b\xaaGq'
>>> pin.decode("utf-8", "strict")
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
UnicodeDecodeError: 'utf-8' codec can't decode byte 0xbf in position 0: invalid start byte
```
I think your Datakey Cryptoki "dkck201" library is not PKCS#11 compliant.

@grawee python-snap7 is just a ctypes wrapper around snap7 c library. 
The python function `db_read()` just calls `Cli_DBRead()` function from c library. 
According to header file (snap7.h) the "start" augment is declared as integer. 
See: https://github.com/SCADACS/snap7/blob/master/examples/cpp/snap7.h#L436

The types must match. Therefore you have to use integer as a start parameter in `db_read()` and read whole byte(s) from PLC. 
You should be able to read individual bits from returned bytearray with `snap7.util.get_bool().` 

See example:
```python
reading = plc.db_read(31, 120, 1)  # read 1 byte from db 31 staring from byte 120
snap7.util.get_bool(reading, 0, 5)  # read a value of fifth bit
```

In the `Class Instances` section of [the standard type hierarchy](https://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#the-standard-type-hierarchy), the docs say this:

> If no class attribute is found, and the object’s class has a __getattr__() method, that is called to satisfy the lookup.

Although it's not pointedly specific, it does imply that the `__getattr__` is always called on the class and not on the instance, which is consistent with your finding. In that case, the technique used by method_cache, setting a callable on the instance, isn't viable for `__getattr__`.

Similarly, the [docs on special method names](https://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#special-method-names) indicate:

> For instance, if a class defines a method named __getitem__(), and x is an instance of this class, then x[i] is roughly equivalent to type(x).__getitem__(x, i).

Again, the docs explicitly indicate that although these methods are typically described as instance methods (with a `self` parameter), they're treated as class methods.

And if we dig into the [Python 2.7 docs on special method lookup for new style classes](https://docs.python.org/2.7/reference/datamodel.html#special-method-lookup-for-new-style-classes), it's clearly stated:

> For new-style classes, implicit invocations of special methods are only guaranteed to work correctly if defined on an object’s type, not in the object’s instance dictionary.

That nuance is [also present in the Python 3 docs](https://docs.python.org/3.5/reference/datamodel.html#special-method-lookup).

Therefore, the technique employed by `method_cache` is simply not viable for special methods.

Ah, I see, ok! That's a very different situation and I think is better handled by using [callable rates](https://django-ratelimit.readthedocs.io/en/latest/rates.html#callables).

The idea is that you set the rate to a callable, say a function `get_api_rate`, which gets both the "group" of the ratelimit and the request object. Then you can check the user and somehow look up what their limit should be, e.g.:

```py
def get_api_rate(group, request):
    max_for_user = get_max_for_user(request.user)
    return (max_for_user, 3600)  # for a rate per hour

# ... snip

@ratelimit(rate=get_api_rate)
def my_api_method(request):
    # do your thing
    pass
```

does that make sense?

This gives you a lot more flexibility, too. Specifying the user cost would limit you to a number of ratios (e.g. if the limit is 100, a user can make 100, or 50, or 33, or 25 calls, but never 75 or 66). But specifying a per-user rate lets you use any numbers now or in the future.

There's an open ticket (#123) to document an example of how to do this.

I had a look at my HASS UI, and indeed it's not possible to change entity ID's via the UI. So I guess there has to be something done to provide that functionality. What that is I don't know yet.

The main problem I see is that with HomeMatic we allow two modes: _regular_ and _resolvenames_. The regular mode would be ideal for compatibility with the entity registry because the IDs always are unique already. But the _resolvenames_ mode is different, and theoretically an entity ID can be the same for different devices if the name has changed on the CCU.

So in my opinion to support the entity registry we would have to get rid of the _resolvenames_ mode, or if possible provide registry-compatibility depending on which mode the user has chosen. The big problem is, that maybe a lot of users (for good reasons) like the _resolvenames_ mode because it makes it easier getting started. But in the worst case this prohibits us from using the entity registry because we would break the setups and automations of many users, which could even be a security issue if locks are used or sensors that trigger an alarm system.

I will have to do some research regarding the entity registry. Of course anyone who knows more about the topic than me can provide information here.

Note - I would call this a port request rather than issue.

The short - I'd love to hear if any other collaborators are working on this. I'd like to help.

The long - 

My work project team is trying to implement the BetaGeoFitter class in PySpark and that's exactly as far as we've gotten - convert the pyspark dataframe to pandas dataframe and run the methods on the pandas dataframe. This means we're single-threading each iteration because we've not been able to get the numpy methods underlying the pandas methods to parallelize.

We have tried a UDF to import lifetimes and requirements to each node when we call the method, we have tried recoding portions of the BetaGeoFitter class to be acceptable to PySpark syntax, but it ultimately comes back to methods and functions that expect an ndarray or pandas series but instead get a PySpark column object. We even took a barebones approach and focused on the fitting algorithm, but the two required functions - log() and gammaln() - are problematic in PySpark. Log() in PySpark expects a float, gammaln() is not currently defined for PySpark but rather exists in Scala and lacks a JVM wrapper to be comprehensible to the PySpark interpreter.  

I know of a related project - sparkit-learn - that has some promise of porting these methods, however their project is not currently on our distro (Anaconda) and I'm not sure the extent to which their latest release will work with our PySpark instance.

I believe this is supported under the `single-avoid-escape` and `double-avoid-escape` options for the `string-quote` option, that is to say, given the python file `test.py`

```py
X = 'my standard style'     # line 1
Y = "not standard style"    # line 2
Z = "this isn't a warning"  # line 3
```

and the standard configuration, we should see something like:

```console
$ pylint --load-plugins pylint_quotes test.py 
No config file found, using default configuration
************* Module test
C:  2, 0: Invalid string quote ", should be ' (invalid-string-quote)
C:  3, 0: Invalid string quote ", should be ' (invalid-string-quote)
C:  1, 0: Missing module docstring (missing-docstring)

------------------------------------------------------------------
Your code has been rated at 0.00/10 (previous run: 3.33/10, -3.33)
```
Here, we'll ignore the missing module docstring, but note that lines 2 and 3 generated a warning for invalid string quote.

now, with the same `test.py` file, we can add a `.pylintrc` file for specifying the configuration. If we want single quotes as the default, but to not fail if double quotes are used to avoid escaping, we should use `single-avoid-escape`, e.g.

```ini
[BASIC]

string-quote=single-avoid-escape
```

Now, running it again, we see something like:
```console
$ pylint --load-plugins pylint_quotes test.py 
Using config file /Users/edaniszewski/tmp/.pylintrc
************* Module test
C:  2, 0: Invalid string quote ", should be ' (invalid-string-quote)
C:  1, 0: Missing module docstring (missing-docstring)

------------------------------------------------------------------
Your code has been rated at 3.33/10 (previous run: 3.33/10, +0.00)
```

Here, only line 2 generated a warning. Line 3 (the line that switches quote type to avoid escaping) does not generate a warning.

class MyModel(TranslatableModel):

Hello!

Well, as a rule, an instance method such as `get_slug` should use current language, and only current language. If another language is wished for, the calling code should load it before invoking the method.

There are several options to that end:
* Load the city alongside the foreign object by reversing the query, making `language()` available. This usually means two requests instead of one, yielding two disconnected objects.
* Load the city alongside the foreign object, using `prefetch_related` with a custom queryset. This means two requests instead of one, but the objects will reference each other, which is more convenient if you are accessing them through foreign fields.
* Use a custom query (not recommended as it is likely to break when upgrading Django).

To better appreciate your use case, I would need more details on the code that invokes that `get_slug` method and the queries it runs to load both the city and the foreign object.

import sys
import os
import six
import socket
import websocket

def recv_packet_unicode(self):
    try:
        packet_text = self._connection.recv()
    except websocket.WebSocketTimeoutException as e:
        raise TimeoutError('recv timed out (%s)' % e)
    except websocket.SSLError as e:
        raise ConnectionError('recv disconnected by SSL (%s)' % e)
    except websocket.WebSocketConnectionClosedException as e:
        raise ConnectionError('recv disconnected (%s)' % e)
    except socket.error as e:
        raise ConnectionError('recv disconnected (%s)' % e)
    try:
        encoded = six.b(packet_text)
    except (UnicodeEncodeError):
        # print("six.b latin-l encoding fails, switching to six.u unicode uncoding")
        pass
    else:
        encoded = six.u(packet_text)
    engineIO_packet_type, engineIO_packet_data = parse_packet_text(encoded)
    yield engineIO_packet_type, engineIO_packet_data
    
# Set the new recv_packet_unicode method
WebsocketTransport.recv_packet = recv_packet_unicode

One problem I see is that you are not sleeping inside the loop that delivers log lines to the client. Since you are using gevent, multitasking is cooperative, so you have to call `sleep(0)` to let other tasks run. Among those other tasks there are those that make sure that the queue of messages to clients are serviced.

Oh, I see. Thanks for pointing me to the code. The misunderstanding on your part is that there isn't a `Namespace` instance per client. The namespace object is a singleton, so you cannot keep client state in it, like your `start` attribute. What you need to do is build your own per-client storage, which could be a global dictionary, for example, indexed by the client session id.

You both understand what I want to do and misunderstand me (the later of which is entirely my fault). 

Adding a new request property is well outside the scope, and I don't think that should be included.  In virtually any implementation of this, more app-level hacking is needed (more on that in a the next paragraph).  I'm primarily concerned with making that "doable" with this library.  Having a library that fully supports/automates that would be great, but I'd be content with the ability to easily leverage existing code.   (sidenote: i'd really love for pyramid to support separate http and https sessions itself,  but I lost that battle long ago).

We're doing this now with pyramid_beaker using a rather simple technique, but it requires a trivial manual sync whenever that module is updated (which is, thankfully, never).  I created a new class `ISessionHttpsFactory(ISessionFactory)`, [I think this was because I had to due to pyramid internals, but it could have just been a bad working knowledge of interfaces] and then register it into pyramid in a similar manner to how pyramid implements the regular ISession during a NewRequest event.    The beaker.session code is entirely self-contained, so the only thing I need to keep in sync is some config argument coercion and then I can piggyback off their factory configuration. 

`pyramid_redis_sessions` is built quite a bit differently though.  There are a few points in the code that  specifically address `request.session`, and there's a lot more code in the factory.  IIRC it was mostly in the cookie code.

So stated differently, beaker.session is largely unaware of request.session; pyramid_redis_sessions is somewhat dependent on it -- and what I'd like to do is to make those various lookups more configurable so the package could be more easily repurposed onto alternate request attributes.

@nextghost 

Thanks for the reply.

Aa an FYI, Mongo is in fact capable of doing the equivalent of a subselect using its aggregation pipeline.  It may not be worth the bother for pydal to support that, but it is possible.

The 'query_env' parameter appears to start at the top of the tree through _expand() via expand().  The expand method is only called from SQLAdapter or from specific adapters.  So it does not appear to me that all of Mongo's expand needs this interface change at all.  And to my eye it considerably clutters the code. The string 'query_env' now appears 174 times in a file which only contains 461 lines of code.  And yet 'query_env' is not used once in all of that.

At the larger level in this PR, the string 'query_env' appears 753 times, and yet is only used a very few times.  In an effort to avoid a bunch of boilerplate, the mongo adapter uses the decorators 'needs_aggregation_pipeline()' and 'check_fields_for_cmp()', as well as an attribute '_parse_data' attached to Expression, Query and their children, to track some meta data without needing to modify (clutter) dozens of parse functions that have no need to understand the larger context. A similar mechanism might allow this PR to perform the same function as the current suggested code, without so much of an impact on non-associated source code.

Thanks again.

@gi0baro @niphlod @mdipierro 

So to summarize the above:

I would strongly suggest that the extensive changes to Mongo driver with the addition of 'query_env' are not warranted.  

I would also (weakly) suggest that 'query_env' could be made an attribute of Expression and Query to allow it to be accessed in the depths of the parse tree without needing to be passed around explicitly to so many places where it does not actually matter.

I think we should have the following flow:
- MKL-DNN enabled: MKL-OP -> Native-OP -> Fail
- MKL-DNN disabled: Native-OP -> Fail

This flow allows us to dismiss the fact whether MKLDNN is present or not. If an operator is missing in MKL as well as in the native implementation, we're free to skip that part (and print a message). 

You can always use this context information to determine whether an absent operator is actually a true failure because it's expected to be available or whether we're fine with skipping. Something you seem to miss here is the fact that we also want to test how MXNet behaves if an operator is used which is unsupported by MKLDNN by supported by our native implementation. Right now, you're only targeting your covered use-case, but we want to cover everything. 

What you basically would have to change is the following: Put all dtypes and test them in a loop. Add a try-catch within that loop and add a handler in that catch. In that handler you check which dtype is currently being tested and whether a failure (OperatorMissingError only!) is fine to be skipped or whether it's expected to be there.

This could look as follows (pseudo-code):
```
for dtype in dtypes:
    try:
        run_test(...)
    catch:
        if context == cpu_native:
            if dtype == int8 || or dtype == float32
                logging.info(skipping due to unsupported dtype {}, dtype)
                continue
        elseif context == cpu_mkldnn:
...
```

Of course, this is a lot of boilerplate code, so it should be put into a separate function and blabla - you know the drill.


Imagine you are currently in the test for uint8 but MKLDNN is not present. This means we can only test the native implementation. Since there is no native implementation, we would simply skip that test and continue with the next dtype. If MKLDNN is present, the test will be executed and run as usual. 

You should keep the user perspective in mind. They should not care whether our backend supports int8, uint8 or some other datatype. They just want it to work. Thus, we should make sure that we handle fallbacks properly and also test these cases.

To add to this, I have observed it is not only negative index, but it is for any positive out of bound index.

Further assignment to a out of bound index results in non-deterministic behavior. The assignment is either silently ignored or it fails with a SIGSEV or SIGABRT.

silently ignored -
```
arr = mx.ndarray.ones((10,))
arr[np.array([10], dtype=np.int)] = 2
print arr
```

SIGABRT -
```
arr = mx.ndarray.ones((10,))
for i in range(10):
    arr[np.array([10], dtype=np.int)] = 2
print arr
```

SIGSEGV - 

```
arr = mx.ndarray.ones((10,))
for i in range(10):
    arr[np.array([11,12], dtype=np.int)] = [2,2]
print arr
```

For all these cases of assignment as well as retrieval, we should be failing with a index out of bound error , which is the behavior in numpy for these cases.

I'm sorry, but I think you are wrong.  Doing it "by hand" would not be
optimal as using the  c++ ctc_decoder.
The reason is within the c++ api ctc-decoder you have access to the softmax
probabilities for each character in the sequence while using the python api
you have access only the the top N paths of the decoder (causing the beam
search in the first option being much optimal than the second)

1) Unfortunately we have no early stopping implementation yet. For now you have to manually observe your training's loss progress. Would be a great contribution. I'll open an issue for this.
2) At the moment there is no direct code/variable to increase the number of saved checkpoints. One could pass a custom [CheckpointSaverHook](https://www.tensorflow.org/api_docs/python/tf/train/CheckpointSaverHook) into the `MonitoredTrainingSession`. Another PR? I'll open an issue for this.
3) This is the number of `global_step`s - explained [here](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/41166681/what-does-tensorflow-global-step-mean). No, this cannot and should not be changed to epochs, as checkpoint creation is totally independent of epochs. TensorFlow treats checkpoints only time-wise or (global)step-wise. We also want to keep things as TensorFlow'ish as possible and try to avoid code piles on top of it.

You need to call refresh at every `.save` and `.delete` you are calling from your tests, easiest way is to just pass `refresh=True` to the calls.

Also I would recommend just deleting the index in your `tearDown` method. So overall your tests should look like:

    def setUp(self):
        connections.add_connection('default', Elasticsearch())
        Report.init()

    def tearDown(self):
        connections.get_connection.indices.delete(index='my-test-index')

    def test_report_creation(self):
        report = Report(description='My very first report')
        report.save(refresh=True)
        self.assertEqual(report.description, 'My very first report')
        self.assertEqual(len(Report.search().execute().hits), 1)

    def test_elastic_search_model_dict(self):
        report = Report(description='Test as dict')
        result = report.to_dict()
        self.assertEqual(result['description'], 'Test as dict')

Interesting. I did not use Riak CS before. All I can say at this moment is, Boto3 does automatically change endpoint to "your_bucket_name.s3.amazonaws.com" when it sees fit. This is done by botocore/utils.py:fix_s3_host(), registered to event 'before-sign.s3'.

You may, however, force to unregister that handler from that event.

    import boto3
    from botocore.utils import fix_s3_host

    resource = boto3.resource('s3')
    resource.meta.client.meta.events.unregister('before-sign.s3', fix_s3_host)

    # for bucket in resource.buckets.all():
    #     print(bucket.name)

    bucket = resource.Bucket('rayluo')
    # print(bucket)

    boto3.set_stream_logger('botocore')
    for o in bucket.objects.all():
        print(o)
        break
    print('done')

Bear in mind that, you may or may not still run into other issues. YMMV.

For reference, a quick way to delete all (non-versioned) objects in a bucket using resource models:
```python
import boto3

bucket = boto3.resource('s3').Bucket('bucketname')
bucket.objects.all().delete()
bucket.delete()
```

There is also a versioned objects collector that would allow you to do something similar for versioned objects, but do be careful as that may end up making a lot of requests.

I'm not sure about `get_session()` versus `Session()`, but one thing that I discovered is that aws_access_key_id and aws_secret_access_key can't be None, but they can be bogus.  I'm doing something pretty similar to you, and it works for me with fake-s3:

    session = boto3.session.Session(aws_access_key_id='---', aws_secret_access_key='---')
    resource = session.resource('s3', endpoint_url=..., use_ssl=False)
    client = resource.meta.client
    client.meta.events.unregister('before-sign.s3', fix_s3_host)
    client.generate_presigned_url('get_object', Params={'Bucket": ..., 'Key': ...}, ExpiresIn=...)

Yup, definitely a dozen different ways to convert prior to the Django model and even more prior to hitting this library. I went this route because all of the alternatives require writing more code for an IndexEnumField than you would for, say, an IntegerField. Using the IndexEnumField buys you automatic coercion between integers and enums without needing to write a property, but at the cost of requiring coercion logic elsewhere. I can buy type safety as being the reason for this as opposed to using a LaxIndexEnumField which is explicitly less type safe. It appears that all of the suggestions have drawbacks ranging from type safety to code bloat.

I'll wait to hear back about what route we want to go before making further changes to the PR.

@mrh1997 just because it's not in the pep 508 standard which governs what values we allow in the `requires` section for python -- There _is_ a pep 508 marker that includes this information which is `platform_machine` but we don't support it in the pipfile spec currently. The `--verbose` flag added in this PR is only triggered if put before the command (i.e. between `pipenv` and `run`, or immediately after `run`), and does not eat flags passed to the command. A `--verbose` flag passed to the command also does not trigger the Click flag.

For the off-chance there is a command called `-v` or `--verbose`, you can do `pipenv run -- --verbose ...`, and Click does the right thing. As far as I can tell all the based are covered quite well…

On further investigation, it looks like there are actually five required steps to properly vendor an ELF library:

1) Move vendored library into `.libs`
2) Run `patchelf --set-rpath path/to/.libs OBJ` on all the objects that link to the vendored library
3) Rename the library, e.g. from `libfoo.so.1` to `libfoo-<unique>.so.1`
4) Run `patchelf --replace-needed libfoo.so.1 libfoo-<unique>.so.1 OBJ` on all the objects that link to the vendored library, so that they look for the new name.
5) Crucial, but not at all obvious: Run `patchelf --set-soname libfoo-<unique>.so.1 .libs/libfoo-<unique>.so.1` so that the renamed library knows what its new name is. (The DT_SONAME attribute is what's checked when deciding whether to short-circuit later library lookups, so this is crucial -- if we didn't change this, then when someone else came along looking for `libfoo.so.1` then it would think that the already-loaded `libfoo-<unique>.so.1` satisfied that.)

Another note for the next time this gets worked on (hopefully tomorrow): @kbourgoin's main complaint about the design of this class so far is that batching across calls to `produce` is not supported. We need to be able to specify a batch size - the size of each message batch sent per request to kafka. This batch will build up in memory across multiple calls to `produce` and will send either when the batch size has been reached or a message in the batch has been waiting for a long enough time.
The current implementation does support batching, but only when all of the messages in the batch were supplied in the same `produce` call. This will be my primary focus when I work on this next, which I hope happens all day tomorrow.

@mgennaro Yes, the default for the background objects has `sigma=3` and `iters=5`, see here:  https://github.com/astropy/photutils/blob/master/photutils/background/core.py#L159

Hopefully this isn't too much object-oriented programming.  The background classes all have examples that show how it's used:  http://photutils.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api/photutils.background.MMMBackground.html#photutils.background.MMMBackground

I also plan to add some narrative docs for the new background classes.

As a simple example, instead of:
```
bkg = MedianBackground(sigma=3, iters=5)
```

One would do:
```
bkg = MedianBackground(SigmaClip(sigma=3, iters=5))
```

or

```
bkg = MMMBackground(sigma_clip=SigmaClip(sigma=3, iters=5))
```


One advantage of this is that the `SigmaClip` class has the full suite of `sigma_clip` options (e.g. `sigma_lower`, `sigma_upper`, `cenfunc`, `stdfunc`) whereas previously the background classes did not.

I disagree with the conclusions you've made here.  The functionality that allows for specifying `[capnp]` in the pip command has been there for a long time.  I merely suggested that using the status quo functionality is better than duplicating a version number in a build script.

To @scottpurdy's point, there is some duplication between nupic.core and nupic and this changeset further diverges the two projects with respect to how the `pycapnp` dependency is handled.

re: `get-pip.py`, I suspect it has a short-lived future.  I know @scottpurdy wants it gone, so having a disagreement about it certainly doesn't help, but here goes the rationale: `get-pip.py` was included because it is the recommended way of installing `pip` according to official documentation, and it works consistently across platforms without having to keep track of platform-specific installation techniques.  Bundling it in the repo was done to avoid intermittent failures or security issues related to downloading it every time we run an automated build.

Also, just because something is in the documentation, doesn't mean it makes sense in context.  If we're going to download specific versions of pip, setuptools, and wheel in every build, that certainly adds support to the argument for its removal (i.e., it's redundant).

I did that for another project before and it worked fine. There are some things to keep in mind when doing this:
 1. **Performance:** Avoid nested/hijacked serialization methods. Bad example: Serialize your custom type to a Python dict and that one to JSON and that one to a string (which then is msgpack compatible).
 2. **Security:** whatever you do, **do NOT use (c)Pickle or marshal** to serialize your custom type. Please also try to avoid pulling more C libraries as good as you can, because they are one of the most targeted parts when it comes to serialization exploits. Do not load or execute modules or methods just because a message tells you to do so (that's basically why cPickle is insecure).
 3. **Stability:** There is no way to figure out which module version submitted a specific message part to the broker and I hope that nobody will attach all that metadata to every single message. If you introduce a new type to the serializer, make sure it is stable enough to survive the next 10 year.
 4. **Usage:** There are only 128 slots, so use this workaround rarely. If you can, put a warning if we exceed 64 slots. Whenever you can, use a JSON-like structure for your object (i.e. dicts+lists+strings+numbers+booleans+...) which then is handled by msgpack without a custom type.
 5. **Slot Allocation:** How do you plan to solve this without the need that every module knows all slots allocations of every other module AND the satisfaction of *3. Stability*? Keep in mind that we plan to separate modules from the core so that they are working independently (except of dependency relations of course)

# Update a policy:
qds.py --url="https://api.qubole.net/api" --token="$API_TOKEN" cluster object_policy update --cluster-id 201 --policy "[{\"access\": \"allow\", \"action\": [\"all\"], \"condition\": {\"qbol_users\": [55]} }]"
{
    "message": "Policy saved successfully.",
    "id": 2,
    "success": true
}

#get a policy
qds.py --url="https://api.qubole.net/api" --token=“$AUTH_TOKEN” cluster object_policy get --cluster-id 201
{
    "policy": [
        {
            "access": "allow",
            "action": [
                "all"
            ],
            "condition": {
                "qbol_users": [
                    55
                ]
            }
        }
    ],
    "id": 1
}



Sample python script to update and get a policy. Output will be similar to the one above.
========================================================================
from qds_sdk.object_policy import ObjectPolicy
from qds_sdk.cluster import Cluster
from qds_sdk.qubole import Qubole
import json

token = “YOUR_TOKEN”
Qubole.configure(api_token=token, version="latest", api_url="https://api.qubole.net/api")
policy = """  [{\"access\": \"allow\", \"action\": [\"all\"], \"condition\": {\"qbol_users\": [55]} }]  """
print ObjectPolicy.update_object_policy(201, Cluster.__name__, policy)
print ObjectPolicy.get_object_policy(201, Cluster.__name__)
========================================================================

Ah, no, I missed that, but it's just an issue with the numpy array printing rounding off the output. Floats do follow rules, you don't just randomly get `a < b` returning the wrong result because `a` is only a *little* bit bigger than `b` :-).

However, this means that my suggestion of `print`ing the value isn't going to produce any useful output either, because numpy will round them off for display again :-(. So actually, can you try:

```python
np.set_printoptions(precision=20)
print(inner_knots)
print(knot_quantiles)
```

?

It definitely would, Config file needs to be changed accordingly. Or better to keep something in configuration file that changes rarely, for example dns name (A or SRV record)

> we would still want the configuration file to represent reality or we would run into trouble when trying to recover from a failure where we would no longer have access to the runtime member cache.

Indeed, config file must be in sync with reality. We are using SRV discovery for that. When etcd cluster chages it's topology, it automatically updates SRV and A records.

It's actually not hard to implement
```yaml
etcd:
  hosts:
    - host1:2379
    - host2:2379
    - host3:2379
```

But you still have to keep this configuration in sync.

I agree the documentation is lacking on how to use Cognito. I think part of the confusion is because of the distinction between using Chalice for web based API access and the expectation of Chalice endpoints being used in the middle of a workflow that already has something like a Cognito JWT.

So to hopefully give an example of what I've gotten to work, my current approach has been to setup a test user and run something like:

    from warrant import Cognito
    import requests
    
    # use warrant to authenticate a test user
    u = Cognito('XXX_YOUR_USER_POOL_ID', 'XXX_YOUR_CLIENT_ID', username='test_user')
    u.authenticate(password='testtest')
    
    # now you can hit authorized endpoints from chalice that are backed by the cognito pool
    r = requests.get('https://YOUR_CHALICE_REST_API_ID.execute-api.YOUR_AWS_REGION.amazonaws.com/api/',  headers={'Authorization': u.id_token})
    print r.text

I think Chalice was meant to be placed into a workflow. By supporting the Cognito authorizer it allows for endpoints to be protected and return an appropriate API result based on the request.

@jjhelmus @meteoswiss-mdr 

The problem has to do with indexing and the @meteoswiss-mdr code is missing something. 

The thing is, that the `rayinfo` can hold several types of info. Normally this is just the `startangle`. In some systems the `stopangle` is added. The provided data from @srbrodzik contains 6 of them (`startangle`, `stopangle`, 'timestamp`, `txpower`, `noisepowerh` and `noisepowerv`). 

As the current code just checks for two elements (`startangle`, `stopangle`) it wrongly assumes just one element (because there are 6) and is trying to read it. The proposed change by @jjhelmus assumes that the `startangle` is always in the first element. But this will let the `stopangle` unacknowledged, although it is there. And it will break the reader if the `rayinfo` contains only the `startangle`.

So a more sophisticated check is needed.

Just as a side note for devs and users. For getting a quick overview of the layout of a deeply nested python dictionary (as the wradlib rainbow reader is putting out) I recommend using `traitsui`:

http://wradlib.org/2016/03/treeview-with-traits/

Hi @hiranya911,
I looked into this, and the `ImportError` is actually coming from grpc, which is issuing `import enum`.

`enum` was added to the standard library in Python 3.4, _but_ there is a backport on PyPI for previous versions (`pip install enum34`). If you install `enum34` from pip when using Python 3.3, things might work.

What I mean by "things might work" -- doing so will get you past that particular `ImportError`. I do not guarantee everything will work past that, since we do not officially support or test on Python 3.3. (Your chances are decent that everything will work fine, though.)

```python
    def tearDown(self):
        retry = RetryErrors(NotFound, max_tries=10)
        for doomed in self.to_delete:
            retry(doomed.delete)()
        logging.getLogger().handlers = self._handlers_cache[:]
```

Using `max_tries=10` means we can have a total sleep of

```
1 + 2 + ... + 256 + 512 = 1024 - 1
```

**Oh, man.** Like a half-starved triathlete subsisting on vegan nut butter and dreams, you went the distance. You delivered a full-bodied solution integrating our half-crazed application-specific decorator – complete with formalized unit tests that actually all pass.

It pays to be punny.

## Feature Request Part II: The Legend of Brutal Pain Continues

You're probably going to hate me for this, but my only question now is: *"Now how do I do that recursively over the entire object graph of objects reachable from the root object to be pickled?"* 

Actually, even that was a lie. I have a second question, which is even worse: *"Now how do I make that space- and time-performant?"*

While recursively traversing the object graph certainly seems feasible, preserving and restoring filtered attributes for all objects in that graph seems... *costly.* A two-dimensional `dict` of `dict`s mapping from object IDs to `dict`s mapping from removed attribute names to values would probably do it, but... **urgh.**

Actually, scratch that. In our case, cached properties are all safely deletable; on the next access, they'll simply be transparently recreated. So, the recursive traversal can be simplified by directly deleting all object attributes whose names are prefixed by `__property_cached_` without attempting to preserve and restore those attributes. *Phew!*

That's nice. But I'm still scared. This has become the Mount Doom of `dill` feature requests.

Please don't feel obligated to cobble together another awesome solution. Time is precious. I've consumed enough of yours, already.

## Feature Request Part III: A Whole New World

The fact that I'm now attempting to perform a recursive breadth- or depth-first visitation of every node of an object graph merely to avoid pickling cached attributes tells me I'm squarely in FUBAR Land™. I'm getting that itching, scabrous feeling on my neck again. Something has gone horribly wrong.

There *must* be a better way. My neck deserves better.

Fortunately, `pickle` and `dill` already provide an out-of-the-box solution for filtering attributes: the type-specific `__getstate__()` protocol.  The dwindling voice of reason inside me suggests that I should probably just work within the confines of that elegant API rather than attempting to brute-force recursive traversal into a `Pickler` replacement.<sup>_...as much fun as that would surely be. Don't get me wrong here._</sup>

In short, I'm considering refactoring our `@property_cached` decorator. Currently, this decorator decorates the passed function with *another* function caching properties onto the parent object directly. That doesn't help us. The "...caching properties onto the parent object directly" part is the problem. We can't reasonably implement a universal `__getstate__()` method filtering out cached properties from all possible objects.

At this point, your head is probably planted firmly onto the keyboard. Drool and spittle are beginning to collect in the adjustable ergonomic keyboard tray. But what if it didn't have to be this way?

What if this decorator instead decorated the passed function with a callable class implementing the `__call__()`, `__get__()`, and `__getstate__()` protocols to cache properties onto *itself* rather than the parent object? Specifically:

* `__call__()` will cache the value returned by the decorated function onto the bound method returned by `__get__()`.
* `__get__()` will create and return a bound method, satisfying the descriptor protocol also implemented by the `property` descriptor. Hence, we won't need to decorate this callable class with `property`. <sup>_That's nice, dear._</sup>
*  `__getstate__()` will exclude the value cached onto that bound method. *Hmmm.* **Wait.** That means that `__getstate__()` needs to be defined on the bound method instead, doesn't it? Jesus-on-chocolate-stick, preserve us. I suppose I could define a new `MethodType` subclass implementing `__getstate__()` to exclude the value cached onto itself, but... *urgh.*

I'm perfectly confident that could maybe work.

@levlitichev and I were just talking about whether the (current) list of dictionaries structure is really the best data structure to use for these. So that we're all on the same page/can discuss more widely, the pros and cons we found were:  

Pros (keep it the way it is): 
- Compatible with other code in pestle already 
- Not thaaaat complicated 
- Easy to add, because it for the most part was already written 

Cons (change it): 
- I think that, given that a GMT is a unique ID pointing to (1) a list of genes and (2) a description of the gene set, it would make more logical sense for a GMT to be structured as a dict where keys are UIDs and values are a tuple (for instance, where 0 = gene list and 1=description). > However, what if I want to create multiple subnets, for multiple applications, placing them in as many AZs as I can?

Short answer is, you can't. At least not very easily. CloudFormation exposes the [GetAZs intrinsic function](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSCloudFormation/latest/UserGuide/intrinsic-function-reference-getavailabilityzones.html) but you can't really use this in a CloudFormation template to dynamically create multiple subnets.

If you really want to do this, you would need to resolve AZ's per region/account outside of stacker, and then pass it down as variables to the VPC blueprint. This could potentially be made easier if we exposed [DescribeAvailabilityZones](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSEC2/latest/APIReference/API_DescribeAvailabilityZones.html) as a lookup plugin.

In our experience, doing this dynamically usually creates more problems than it's worth (and GetAZs is potentially harmful, since it's return value is not always determinstic).

It's actually due to how python interprets your code:

```
>>> x = """
... This is
... some
... user data
... """
>>>
>>>
>>> print x

This is
some
user data

>>> x
'\nThis is\nsome\nuser data\n'
>>>
```

You're literally putting new lines in the variable, which is how python interprets it.  The python syntax you want to use is something like:

```
>>> x = ("This is "
... "some "
... "user data"
... )
>>> print x
This is some user data
>>> x
'This is some user data'
```

Hope that helps!

I've been thinking about tackling this, but I can't decide how/whether to handle manylinux compatibility at buildtime. At runtime, you can just use the PEP513 (or modified pip) algorithm, but that won't work for *building* a Linux PEX on an OS X host. The best I've thought of is using `_manylinux.manylinux1_compatible` and only applying that portion of the compatibility check:

> To handle potential future incompatibilities, we standardize a mechanism for a Python distributor to signal that a particular Python install definitely is or is not compatible with `manylinux1` : this is done by installing a module named `_manylinux` , and setting its `manylinux1_compatible` attribute. We do not propose adding any such module to the standard library -- this is merely a well-known name by which distributors and installation tools can rendezvous. However, if a distributor does add this module, *they should add it to the standard library rather* than to a `site-packages/` directory, because the standard library is inherited by virtualenvs (which we want), and `site-packages/` in general is not.

Similar to https://github.com/pypa/pip/issues/3689.

Or is it reasonable to assume `manylinux1` compatibility when building?

I understand the fear of violating the spirit of sacreBLEU and for that reason I included the warning message. BTW: SacreBLEU allows `--refs`, which can lead to similar problems - if someone uses a slightly different version of wmt14 references provided via `--refs`, the only rescue is reporting the signature where you can see that `test.wmt14` is missing and take the result with a grain of salt. Similarly, you can see `tok.none` in the signature.
My use case: someone [reported](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensor2tensor/pull/611#issuecomment-379687796) that [t2t-bleu](https://github.com/tensorflow/tensor2tensor/blob/master/tensor2tensor/bin/t2t_bleu.py) (which should be equivalent to `sacrebleu -tok intl`) gives very different results than `multi-bleu.perl` on a test set which was indeed tokenized. I suggested to use sacreBLEU with `-tok 13a`, `-tok intl` and `-tok none` in order to explore what is the reason behind the difference (theoretically it could be also smoothing). My goal was to show this way why sacreBLEU is much better than multi-bleu.perl for public reports because it does the tokenization automatically by default, so my goal was to promote the spirit of sacreBLEU.:-)

Hi Goliard, please use StackOverflow (with tag 'python-docx') for user support questions. This issues list is reserved for feature requests and suspected bugs.

Okay, I don't completely understand your code for the "finding the ids" bit, but I'm thinking the solution is simpler than you think, basically that we use XPath to find the ids when we need them (and that we don't store them anywhere outside the XML).

I'm also not sure we need an "unclosed" test. I'm open to argumentation to the contrary, but I'm not seeing it yet. It looks to me like there are a certain number of `w:bookmarkStart` elements in the XML, each one represents a bookmark, and a bookmark can be either open or closed. We don't need to determine whether it's open or not unless someone cares to ask or if we need to do an operation that only works on a closed bookmark. We can certainly have an `.is_closed` property on `Bookmark` if we need it (I don't see yet that we do though) and we may very well want a `._bookmarkEnd` property on `Bookmark`, although I haven't seen the use case for that yet (I suspect it's there).

I do think we're on the wrong side of YAGNI here, working with methods and properties we don't have a clear need for yet. At times like these, I always return to the use-case/protocol part of the analysis and narrow my focus down to something entirely concrete. Basically answer the question "As a developer using python-docx, I need to be able to {do what exactly?}"

Anyway, back to the "finding the bookmarkEnd elements" question.

So say we have a `bookmarkStart` and we want to find its matching `bookmarkEnd`. First of all, we should only do this in the oxml layer (not the proxy layer) and we'd do it something like this:

```python
class CT_BookmarkStart(...):

    @property
    def matching_bookmarkEnd(self):
        """Return first `w:bookmarkEnd` element having matching id, None if not present."""
        root_element = self.getroottree().getroot() 
        matching_bookmarkEnds = root_element.xpath(
            './/w:bookmarkEnd[@w:id=\'%d\']' % self.id
        )
        if not matching_bookmarkEnds:
            return None
        return matching_bookmarkEnds[0]
```

@brawer The reason why fontmake has a converter from UFO structures for kerning and mark positioning is that in some cases, it's reasonable to have the higher-level structures which are more editable (kerning.plist etc.), but the "kern" and "mark" features are much more capable than what the FontLab Studio 5 simplified model withthe "top"/"_top" anchor names, or the UFO group kerning model can define. 

In "kern",  you can have contextual kerning and multiple lookups, in "mark" you can also have contextual positionings or such that e.g. modify the base glyph width when placing very wide marks. 

kerning.plist is merely a simple partial template for GPOS. There is a need for competing logics as to how to generate GPOS from UFO, because UFO is not a full description of OpenType, just a higher-level subset that describes the most common scenarios. UFO never had been particularly optimized for non-Latin (which is e.g. why horizontal LTR kerning has its dedicated structure bukerning in other directions does not). 

Out of OpenType's 8 glyph definition formats (glyf, CFF, CFF2, SVG, bdat/EBDT, sbix, CBDT), UFO only provides higher-level structures for the first two. It is a wrapper that works reasonably well for Latin+Cyrillic+Greek fonts with monochrome PostScript outlines, but less so for anything else. This probably is a good thing, otherwise it would have been far too complicated.
